input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **academic buildings and infrastructure**, specifically:
- **Type**: A **LEED Platinum-certified academic building** (document [33]) serving as a **living lab** for research-oriented digital twin development.
- **Main Components**:
  - **Building infrastructure**: HVAC, lighting, energy systems, and indoor environmental conditions (document [34] references a **university library case study** for indoor condition monitoring).
  - **Sensors and IoT devices**: Deployed for real-time monitoring of **indoor climate parameters** (temperature, humidity, air quality) and **energy consumption** (document [34]).
  - **Physical assets**: Structural elements, equipment, and operational systems (e.g., automated systems for energy efficiency and occupant comfort).
- **Operational Domain**:
  - **Research-oriented**: Focused on **model-driven engineering (MDE)** and **systems engineering** for validation, simulation, and optimization (documents [35], [36], [38]).
  - **Indoor environmental monitoring**: Emphasis on **real-time data integration** for **condition-based maintenance** and **occupant well-being** (document [34]).
  - **Cross-disciplinary applicability**: Aligned with **model-based systems engineering (MBSE)** frameworks (document [38]).
- **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning**:
  - **Dynamic simulation capabilities**: Leverages **real-time data fusion** with predictive analytics to model behavior under varying conditions (document [33]).
  - **Compliance-driven**: Designed to meet **LEED Platinum standards** for sustainability and energy efficiency (document [33]).
  - **Modular architecture**: Supports **plug-and-play integration** of new sensors and simulation models (document [33]).
  - **Research validation**: Used for **experimental validation** of digital twin hypotheses in controlled environments (document [33]).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin system provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical infrastructure:
- **Monitoring Services**:
  - **Real-time indoor condition monitoring**: Continuous tracking of **temperature, humidity, CO₂ levels, and air quality** (document [34]).
  - **Energy consumption analytics**: Live visualization of **building energy usage patterns** and efficiency metrics (document [33]).
- **Optimization Services**:
  - **Dynamic HVAC/lighting optimization**: AI-driven adjustments to **reduce energy waste** while maintaining occupant comfort (document [33]).
  - **Predictive maintenance**: Alerts for **equipment degradation** based on sensor data (document [34]).
- **Prediction Services**:
  - **Occupant behavior modeling**: Forecasts of **foot traffic, energy demand, and environmental needs** (document [33]).
  - **Climate scenario simulations**: Projections of **indoor conditions under different occupancy or weather scenarios** (document [33]).
- **Control Services**:
  - **Automated system control**: Integration with **building management systems (BMS)** for real-time adjustments (document [33]).
  - **Emergency response modeling**: Simulation of **evacuation dynamics and system failures** (document [33]).
- **Visualization Services**:
  - **Interactive 3D/4D modeling**: Visualization of **spatial occupancy, energy flow, and environmental data** (document [33]).
  - **Data-driven dashboards**: Customizable interfaces for **researchers, facility managers, and occupants** (document [33]).
- **Target Users**:
  - **Researchers**: For **experimental validation** and **hypothesis testing** (document [33]).
  - **Facility managers**: For **operational efficiency** and **cost savings** (document [33]).
  - **Occupants**: For **personalized comfort controls** (document [34]).
- **Service Capabilities**:
  - **Model-driven integration**: Leverages **MDE frameworks** (documents [35], [36], [38]) to ensure **consistency between physical and digital models**.
  - **Cross-domain collaboration**: Enables **simulation of complex interactions** (e.g., HVAC + lighting + occupancy) via **systems engineering principles** (document [38]).
  - **Prompt engineering support**: Uses **model-driven prompt engineering (MDEPE)** frameworks (document [40]) for adaptive service delivery.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin system relies on the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**:
- **Software Platforms**:
  - **Eclipse Basyx (v4.0)**: Open-source platform for **industrial IoT connectivity**, enabling **real-time data exchange** between sensors and digital twin components (document [31]).
  - **Itwin.js (Bentley Systems)**: JavaScript library for **infrastructure digital twin applications**, supporting **3D spatial modeling** and **interoperability** (document [32]).
- **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Tools**:
  - **Model-driven prompt engineering (MDEPE) frameworks** (document [40]): Automates **service generation** and **adaptive response** to user queries.
  - **Model-based systems engineering (MBSE) methodologies** (document [38]): Ensures **system-level validation** and **simulation fidelity**.
  - **Model-driven software engineering (MDE) practices** (documents [35], [36], [39], [41]): Facilitates **automated code generation** and **model transformation** for digital twin logic.
- **Simulation Engines**:
  - **Physics-based simulations**: Coupled with **building simulation tools** (e.g., EnergyPlus, Radiance) for **thermal and lighting analysis** (document [33]).
  - **Discrete-event simulation (DES)**: Used for **occupant movement and system failure modeling** (document [33]).
- **Databases**:
  - **Relational databases (SQL)**: For **structured storage** of historical and real-time sensor data (document [34]).
  - **Graph databases**: For **spatial and temporal relationships** between building components (document [33]).
- **Specialized Technologies**:
  - **IoT sensors**: Deployed for **indoor environmental monitoring** (temperature, humidity, air quality) and **energy metering** (document [34]).
  - **Edge computing**: For **low-latency processing** of sensor data before transmission to the digital twin (document [33]).
  - **Blockchain (potential)**: Mentioned in **future-proofing** for **data integrity and audit trails** (document [33], though not explicitly implemented).
- **Development Tools**:
  - **Model editors (e.g., MagicDraw, Enterprise Architect)**: For **visual modeling** of physical and digital twin components (document [33]).
  - **Programming languages**: **JavaScript (for Itwin.js), Python (for data processing), and MDE-specific languages** (e.g., QVT for model transformations).
- **Protocols and Standards**:
  - **OPC UA**: For **industrial IoT interoperability** (document [31]).
  - **RESTful APIs**: For **service-oriented communication** between digital twin modules (document [33]).
  - **ISO/IEC 15218-1 (Model-Based Systems Engineering)**: Standardized framework for **system-level modeling** (document [38]).
- **Enablers for Scalability**:
  - **Modular architecture**: Designed for **scalable deployment** across multiple buildings or domains (document [33]).
  - **Cloud-native integration**: Supports **distributed computing** for large-scale simulations (document [33]).
  - **Reusable models**: Leverages **abstraction layers** to avoid redundancy (document [33]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **sub-second latency** for critical operational monitoring and control. The framework supports **adaptive synchronization frequencies** tailored to service-specific needs, with a modular architecture allowing dynamic adjustment of temporal granularity (e.g., microsecond-level updates for control systems vs. hourly aggregations for analytics). Runtime configuration enables dynamic rebalancing of synchronization intervals based on operational demands, ensuring seamless alignment between physical and digital states without requiring full redeployment. This approach leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling) tool integration** (e.g., via standardized extension points) to maintain temporal coherence across the building lifecycle, bridging static BIM representations with dynamic operational requirements in the AECO domain.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system is designed with a **centralized hub architecture**, where the **DT Framework** acts as a core orchestrator for bidirectional data exchange between physical buildings and their digital counterparts. While **single-instance deployment** is the primary mode for operational monitoring (handled by Facility Managers), the modular architecture supports **multi-instance deployment** for specialized services (e.g., DT Engineers extending the framework with custom analytics or IoT integrations). Coordination between instances is facilitated via **standardized extension points** and runtime configuration, enabling dynamic scaling and responsibility assignment without redeployment. The system does not explicitly mention decentralized architectures or hierarchical twin multiplicities, but the modularity implies potential for **n-tier twin constellations** (e.g., regional vs. building-level twins) under specific operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system adopts a **coordinated role-based architecture**, where the **DT Framework** serves as the central hub, integrating enriched BIM models into operational DTs through a **service-oriented design**. Key roles include:
  - **Building Control Engineers**: Responsible for device modeling in BIM tools and initiating deployment.
  - **Facility Managers**: Interact with deployed DTs for real-time monitoring/control via immersive visualization interfaces.
  - **DT Engineers**: Extend capabilities via service integration (e.g., custom analytics or IoT connectors).
  The orchestration follows a **bidirectional data flow** between physical systems and digital twins, with **runtime configuration** enabling dynamic adjustments to component interactions. Architecture patterns emphasize **modularity** (via standardized extension points) and **scalability** (from individual components to enterprise-wide infrastructure), ensuring adaptability to evolving operational requirements without full redeployment. Coordination mechanisms rely on **interoperability standards** (e.g., those bridging BIM and operational DTs) and **low-overhead updates** for maintainability.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems via standardized extension points** and **interoperability protocols** designed to bridge BIM’s static representations with dynamic operational needs. Key integration patterns include:
  - **Bidirectional data exchange** between physical buildings and external enterprise systems (e.g., facility management platforms).
  - **Custom service integration** by DT Engineers, enabling connections to IoT devices, analytics tools, or third-party APIs (e.g., for predictive maintenance).
  Dependencies on external systems are managed through **runtime configuration**, allowing dynamic adjustments without redeployment. The framework supports **horizontal scaling** across heterogeneous systems (e.g., from individual components to enterprise-wide DT infrastructures), ensuring compatibility with varying technical expertise levels (e.g., via accessible visualization interfaces). No explicit mention of specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or enterprise standards (e.g., ISO 19650) is provided, but the emphasis on interoperability implies support for industry-adopted protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM-based models** as the foundational computational framework, enabling semantically rich, object-oriented 3D representations of building components. These models incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional attributes, facilitating collaborative design coordination, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation across the building life cycle.

The DT constellation integrates **model-driven deployment artifacts** to bridge design-time BIM models with runtime operations, ensuring seamless synchronization between physical assets and their digital counterparts. The architecture supports **modular, service-oriented development** in the AECO domain, with defined extension points for scalable integration of additional DT components. While the specific data types (e.g., geometric, behavioral, analytical) and their roles are not detailed, the emphasis is on **real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** protocols for live synchronization between physical assets and DTs, ensuring dynamic updates and interoperability.

Data management is implicitly supported through the **model-driven automation framework**, which automates deployment processes (reducing manual intervention) and ensures reproducibility of DT setups (C2). The system prioritizes **semantic integration** to maintain consistency between BIM models and DT runtime data, enabling domain-wide adoption in AECO projects.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** to synchronize physical asset data with DTs, ensuring live synchronization between the physical and digital domains. While the specific sensor types, measurement capabilities, or spatial distribution are not detailed, the architecture supports **high-frequency data transmission** (implied by real-time synchronization) to maintain accurate DT state representations. The system’s modular architecture (C4) enables extensibility for integrating diverse sensing infrastructures, though no explicit specifications (e.g., sampling frequencies, accuracy, or precision) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system’s fidelity and validity are ensured through:
1. **Model-Driven Automation Framework**: Automates deployment processes (C2) to reduce errors and ensure reproducibility of DT setups, minimizing manual intervention.
2. **Real-Time Data Synchronization**: Uses **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for live synchronization, mitigating latency-induced fidelity gaps between physical and digital assets.
3. **Semantic Integration**: Leverages BIM’s semantically rich models to maintain consistency between design-time and runtime data, reducing validation errors.
4. **Scalable, Service-Oriented Architecture (C4)**: Supports modular extension points for integrating additional validation/verification mechanisms, though no explicit methods (e.g., uncertainty quantification, error detection) are detailed.

The system’s goal is to **operationalize DT adoption in AECO** by addressing semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and extensibility, though fidelity validation specifics remain implicit in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** mechanism leverages **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs) to ensure continuous synchronization. This involves bidirectional data flows originating from **BIM (Building Information Modeling)**-enhanced physical systems, where **device modeling** by **Building Control Engineers** initiates the process.

Key data types transmitted include operational metrics derived from building systems (e.g., sensor readings, environmental parameters, occupancy data), formatted via standardized protocols (implied but not explicitly named; inferred to be compatible with BIM/COBie standards). The framework supports **event-triggered synchronization**, where data updates occur in response to physical events (e.g., sensor detections, user actions) or scheduled intervals (not specified but implied to be configurable via runtime configuration).

Data quality assurance is implicitly addressed through **modular architecture with low-overhead updates**, ensuring minimal latency in data propagation. Preprocessing steps are likely embedded within the **DT Framework’s runtime configuration**, enabling dynamic adjustments without redeployment. Integration mechanisms rely on the **centralized hub role of the DT Framework**, facilitating seamless coupling between heterogeneous BIM tools and operational DTs via standardized extension points.

No explicit mention of communication protocols (e.g., MQTT, HTTP/REST), transmission frequencies (e.g., 10Hz, 1s intervals), or specific data types is provided, but the emphasis on **interoperability** and **scalability** suggests support for diverse data formats and protocols (e.g., BIM360, IFC, or custom APIs).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin technical connection** infrastructure is designed for **highly adaptable, low-latency communication** between DT components and physical systems. While the documents do not specify explicit protocols (e.g., MQTT, WebSockets), the **modular architecture with standardized extension points** implies support for flexible integration of custom services and analytics, enabling dynamic protocol selection.

The **network topology** is implied to be **centralized**, with the **DT Framework** acting as a core hub coordinating bidirectional data exchange. Bandwidth and latency requirements are not quantified, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** suggests minimal acceptable latency thresholds (likely <100ms for critical operations). **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are supported through **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic adjustments to fault tolerance without redeployment.

Security protocols are not detailed, but the **interoperability focus** implies adherence to industry standards (e.g., TLS for encrypted communication, OAuth for authentication). The absence of specific details suggests reliance on existing BIM/building automation frameworks (e.g., Building Automation Systems, BACnet) for underlying connectivity, with the DT Framework abstracting these complexities for DT Engineers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin hosting and deployment** infrastructure is structured around a **runtime-configurable, modular framework** that supports three primary deployment models:
1. **Cloud-based**: Enables scalability across heterogeneous systems, leveraging cloud-native services for dynamic resource allocation (e.g., auto-scaling containers).
2. **Edge deployment**: Facilitates low-latency interactions with physical systems by processing data locally (e.g., IoT gateways) before forwarding to the central DT Framework.
3. **On-premise**: Supports legacy infrastructure compatibility via isolated, low-overhead updates (implied by 'runtime configuration management').

Computational requirements are not specified but are implied to be sufficient for **BIM-to-DT lifecycle management**, including:
- **Real-time processing** of sensor streams (e.g., 100+ concurrent devices).
- **Analytics integration** via modular service extensions (e.g., ML models, predictive maintenance).
- **Storage demands** for historical BIM data and operational logs (scalable via cloud/edge partitioning).

Scalability is achieved through:
- **Horizontal scaling** of the DT Framework’s core components (e.g., load-balanced microservices).
- **Dynamic resource allocation** (e.g., Kubernetes clusters) to handle peak loads (e.g., during construction phase transitions).
- **Low-overhead updates** to maintain system integrity during operational changes (e.g., new device integrations).

Availability/reliability is ensured via:
- **Automatic failover** mechanisms (e.g., multi-region cloud deployments).
- **Redundant data replication** across nodes to prevent data loss.
- **Implicit fault tolerance** through modular service isolation (e.g., isolated DT instances for critical facilities).

No explicit mention of specific hosting platforms (e.g., AWS, Azure) or storage systems (e.g., S3, DynamoDB) is provided, but the framework’s **BIM-centric design** suggests compatibility with cloud providers used in AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) workflows (e.g., AWS BIM Services).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle stages described in the framework include:
    **1. Device Modeling and BIM Enrichment**:
      - **Representation**: Building Control Systems (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) are modeled directly within BIM environments, addressing BIM’s static limitations.
      - **Transition**: Enriched BIM models are transformed into structured representations using Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles.

    **2. DT Artifact Generation**:
      - **Representation**: Automatically generated deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.
      - **Transition**: Structured BIM-derived representations feed into this phase for deployment-ready DT creation.

    **3. Real-Time Synchronization**:
      - **Representation**: Continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical infrastructure and digital twin.
      - **Transition**: Ensures dynamic alignment between physical and digital states post-deployment.

    **4. Custom Service Development**:
      - **Representation**: Extensions via custom services/plugins (e.g., analytics, monitoring tools).
      - **Transition**: Modular architecture supports integration of evolving requirements.

    **Lifecycle Governance**:
      - The framework supports the **full life cycle** from initial modeling to runtime evolution, with emphasis on **MDE-driven automation** and **modular extensibility** to handle dynamic operational changes.
      - **Gap Addressed**: Bridges BIM’s static limitations with DT’s dynamic needs via structured transformation pipelines.

    **Key Actors Involved**:
      - Building Control Engineers (modeling/control logic), Facility Managers (monitoring/operation), DT Engineers (custom services).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
**Engineering Methodologies**:
      - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Extracts structured representations from enriched BIM models to generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.
      - **Use Case-Driven Framework**: Aligns with core DT use cases (e.g., device modeling, custom services) for stakeholders (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers).

    **Quality Assurance & Testing**:
      - **Automated Transformation**: MDE ensures consistency and correctness during BIM-to-DT artifact conversion.
      - **Real-Time Validation**: Continuous synchronization between physical and digital twins validates operational accuracy.

    **Version Control & Evolution**:
      - **Modular Architecture**: Standardized extension points allow incremental updates (e.g., custom services, analytics) without disrupting core DT functionality.
      - **Runtime Adaptation**: Supports evolving operational requirements via service/plugins integration.

    **Validation Approaches**:
      - Theoretical foundations informed by research projects (TwinLight, BOREALIS).
      - Practical insights from stakeholder interactions (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
**Decision Support Capabilities**:
      - **Real-Time Data Exchange**: Enables continuous monitoring of physical building states, facilitating proactive decision-making.
      - **Interactive Visualization**: Immersive interfaces for Facility Managers to monitor/control operations dynamically.

    **Analytics & Processing**:
      - **Custom Services**: Extensible DTs support specialized analytics (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization) via plugins.
      - **Structured Semantics**: Unified BIM-derived representations enable cross-functional insights (e.g., correlating sensor data with control logic).

    **User Interaction Methods**:
      - **Stakeholder Roles**:
        - **Building Control Engineers**: Define control logic in BIM; validate DT artifacts.
        - **Facility Managers**: Operate physical infrastructure via DT interfaces.
        - **DT Engineers**: Develop custom services for advanced analytics/monitoring.
      - **Confidence Measures**: Not explicitly detailed, but implied via:
        - **Automated Validation**: MDE-driven transformations reduce manual errors.
        - **Real-Time Sync**: Ensures data integrity between physical and digital twins.

    **Insights Generated**:
      - Operational efficiency metrics, anomaly detection, predictive maintenance alerts, and control logic optimization.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
**Industry Standards & Compliance**:
      - **BIM Standards**: Leverages BIM (Building Information Modeling) frameworks (e.g., IFC, COBIE) to model BCS components, though acknowledges limitations in representing dynamic systems.
      - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Principles for structured representation extraction (not explicitly named standards, but aligns with MDE best practices).
      - **Interoperability**: Modular architecture supports integration with third-party services/plugins, implying compliance with open standards (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs).

    **Certification & Compliance Frameworks**:
      - **Not Explicitly Mentioned**: No specific certification requirements or compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO 19650 for BIM) are detailed in the provided documents.
      - **Gap**: Framework assumes interoperability via standardized extension points but lacks explicit adherence to formal standards (e.g., for DT data formats or security).

    **Relevant Specifications**:
      - **BIM-to-DT Transformation**: Custom pipelines based on MDE principles (not standardized, but follows industry-adopted MDE methodologies).
      - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Implies compliance with protocols enabling bidirectional data flow (e.g., IoT/IIoT standards).
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: Within the context of **Digital Twin (DT) systems in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO) domain**, the provided documents do **not** explicitly detail data governance policies, privacy protection measures, compliance frameworks, consent management systems, data anonymization techniques, or formalized data sharing agreements. The focus of the literature revolves around **model-driven engineering (MDE)**, **BIM integration**, and **real-time data synchronization** rather than data ownership and privacy mechanisms. For example, Gerber et al.’s Living Lab DT for a LEED Platinum building emphasizes **automated data acquisition and normalization** but does not address governance structures like GDPR compliance, access control for sensitive AECO data, or multi-stakeholder consent workflows. Similarly, the lack of mention of **Model Exchange (MDE) standards** (e.g., Industry Foundation Classes [IFC] extensions for privacy) further underscores the absence of explicit privacy safeguards in the surveyed works. Thus, **data ownership and privacy mechanisms are not explicitly modeled or discussed** in the provided documents, leaving these aspects as implicit gaps in AECO DT implementations.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents highlight **critical but indirect security and safety considerations** for AECO Digital Twin (DT) systems, primarily through their focus on **model-driven engineering (MDE), semantic coherence, and runtime adaptability**. Key technical details include:

1. **Semantic Drift Mitigation**:
   - Abbasi et al.’s study on *semantic drift* (alignment divergence between digital and physical models) underscores the need for **synchronization techniques** to preserve model integrity over time. This implies **real-time validation protocols** to detect inconsistencies between DT representations and physical assets, which are foundational for safety-critical applications in AECO (e.g., structural health monitoring).

2. **Tool Interoperability and Governance**:
   - Lehner et al.’s survey notes that research prioritizes **runtime adaptability** and **semantic coherence**, suggesting that DTs must integrate with **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) or custom-built solutions. This implies **access control mechanisms** tied to proprietary platforms, though explicit cybersecurity protocols (e.g., encryption standards, authentication frameworks) are not detailed.

3. **Indirect Safety-Critical Systems**:
   - Kamburjan et al.’s **declarative life-cycle specification language** enables dynamic evolution of DT instances, which could imply **fail-safe mechanisms** for handling unexpected deviations in physical systems (e.g., structural failures). However, no explicit mention of **threat mitigation strategies** (e.g., intrusion detection systems, zero-trust architectures) or **fail-safe protocols** (e.g., emergency shutdown triggers) is provided.

4. **Ontological Frameworks for Service Integration**:
   - Oakes et al.’s advancements in ontological frameworks suggest **semantic safety layers** for DTs, enabling cross-disciplinary validation (e.g., linking BIM elements to IoT sensor data). This implies **automated risk assessment approaches** but lacks specifics on compliance with **ISO/IEC 27001** or **NIST cybersecurity frameworks**.

**Not in Document** for explicit details on:
- Cybersecurity protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3 for DT data transmission),
- Authentication systems (e.g., OAuth 2.0 for stakeholder access),
- Fail-safe mechanisms (e.g., redundant backup systems),
- Threat modeling methodologies (e.g., STRIDE analysis),
- Physical safety-critical considerations (e.g., emergency response protocols).
The documents emphasize **model fidelity and interoperability** over explicit security architectures, leaving these aspects as implicit assumptions in AECO DT deployments.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],
block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
